title.zh: 卷九十六下 西域傳
title.en: Volume 96b: Traditions of the Western Regions 2
title.raw: 卷九十六下 西域傳 Volume 96b: Traditions of the Western Regions 2

sentences.zh: 烏孫國，大昆彌治赤谷城，去長安八千九百里。

translations.literal: The state of Wusun: the great Kunmi rules from Chigu city; it is eight thousand nine hundred li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Wusun was ruled by the senior Kunmi from Chigu; the capital lay eight thousand nine hundred li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶十二萬，口六十三萬，勝兵十八萬八千八百人。

translations.literal: Households one hundred twenty thousand; population six hundred thirty thousand; able soldiers one hundred eighty-eight thousand eight hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: It had one hundred twenty thousand households, six hundred thirty thousand people, and one hundred eighty-eight thousand eight hundred men of military age.

sentences.zh: 相，大祿，左右大將二人，侯三人，大將、都尉各一人，大監二人，大吏一人，捨中大吏二人，騎君一人。

translations.literal: A chancellor, a Dalu, two left and right grand generals, three marquises, one each of grand general and commandant, two grand supervisors, one grand clerk, two Shezhong grand clerks, one cavalry lord.
translations.idiomatic: Its government included a chancellor, a grand steward (Dalu), two senior generals of the left and right, three marquises, a grand general and a commandant, two chief overseers, a chief clerk, two chief clerks of the royal encampment, and a master of cavalry.

sentences.zh: 東至都護治所千七百二十一里，西至康居蕃內地五千里。

translations.literal: East to the Protector-General's seat one thousand seven hundred twenty-one li; west to Kangju's inner fan territory five thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: It lay one thousand seven hundred twenty-one li east of the Protector-General's headquarters and five thousand li west of the inner pasturelands of Kangju.

sentences.zh: 地莽平。

translations.literal: The land is broadly level.
translations.idiomatic: The terrain was for the most part open and flat.

sentences.zh: 多雨，寒。

translations.literal: Much rain; cold.
translations.idiomatic: Rainfall was heavy and the climate cold.

sentences.zh: 山多松樠。

translations.literal: In the mountains many pines and oaks.
translations.idiomatic: The hills were thick with pine and oak.

sentences.zh: 不田作種樹，隨畜逐水草，與匈奴同俗。

translations.literal: They do not till fields, plant trees, or sow; they follow livestock in pursuit of water and grass; their customs are the same as the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: They did not farm or plant orchards but moved their herds after water and pasture, in the same nomadic way as the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 國多馬，富人至四五千匹。

translations.literal: The state has many horses; the rich reach four or five thousand head.
translations.idiomatic: Horses were abundant; a wealthy household might own four or five thousand.

sentences.zh: 民剛惡，貪狼無信，多寇盜，最為強國。

translations.literal: The people are stubborn and fierce, greedy and wolflike without good faith, many are bandits and robbers; it is the strongest state.
translations.idiomatic: The people were tough and truculent, rapacious and untrustworthy, and prone to raiding; among the frontier powers Wusun ranked as the strongest.

sentences.zh: 故服匈奴，後盛大，取羈屬，不肯往朝會。

translations.literal: Formerly they submitted to the Xiongnu; afterward they grew great, took halter-rein subordination, and were unwilling to go to court assemblies.
translations.idiomatic: They had once acknowledged Xiongnu overlordship, but as they grew powerful they slipped the tether of vassalage and refused to attend the Xiongnu court.

sentences.zh: 東與匈奴、西北與康居、西與大宛、南與城郭諸國相接。

translations.literal: East it joins the Xiongnu; northwest it joins Kangju; west it joins Dayuan; south it joins the walled-city states.
translations.idiomatic: It bordered the Xiongnu on the east, Kangju to the northwest, Ferghana to the west, and the oasis kingdoms of the Tarim basin on the south.

sentences.zh: 本塞地也，大月氏西破走塞王，塞王南越縣度。

translations.literal: Originally it was Sai territory; the Great Yuezhi in the west defeated and drove away the Sai king; the Sai king crossed the Suspended Crossing southward.
translations.idiomatic: The land had once belonged to the Sai people until the Yuezhi, pressing from the west, shattered their kingdom and forced the Sai king south across the rope bridge gorges.

sentences.zh: 大月氏居其地。

translations.literal: The Great Yuezhi dwelt in that land.
translations.idiomatic: The Great Yuezhi then settled there.

sentences.zh: 後烏孫昆莫擊破大月氏，大月氏徙西臣大夏，而烏孫昆莫居之，故烏孫民有塞種、大月氏種雲。

translations.literal: Afterward the Wusun Kunmo attacked and defeated the Great Yuezhi; the Great Yuezhi moved west and submitted to Bactria, while the Wusun Kunmo dwelt there; therefore the Wusun people have Sai stock and Great Yuezhi stock, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Later the Wusun ruler Kunmo broke the Yuezhi, who withdrew westward to subjugate Bactria while the Kunmo took their pastures—hence the saying that Wusun blood mixed Sai and Yuezhi strains.

sentences.zh: 始張騫言烏孫本與大月氏共在敦煌間，今烏孫雖強大，可厚賂招，令東居故地，妻以公主，與為昆弟，以制匈奴。

translations.literal: At first Zhang Qian said Wusun originally together with the Great Yuezhi was between Dunhuang; now though Wusun is strong and great, it can be lavishly bribed and summoned, ordered to dwell east in the old lands, given a princess as wife, made elder and younger brother with us, thereby to restrain the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian had first argued that Wusun and the Yuezhi had once shared the lands around Dunhuang; even now that Wusun was powerful, rich gifts might draw them back east to their old pastures, a Han princess could seal a fraternal alliance, and together they could pin down the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 語在《張騫傳》。

translations.literal: The words are in the 'Traditions of Zhang Qian.'
translations.idiomatic: The full account appears in the biography of Zhang Qian.

sentences.zh: 武帝即位，令騫繼金幣住。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu took the throne, he ordered Qian to continue gold and silk currency stay.
translations.idiomatic: After Emperor Wu came to the throne he sent Zhang Qian west again with another train of gold and silk.

sentences.zh: 昆莫見騫如單于禮，騫大慚，謂曰：「天子致賜，王不拜，則還賜。

translations.literal: The Kunmo received Qian with the rites due a Chanyu; Qian was greatly ashamed and said to him: 'The Son of Heaven sends gifts; if the king does not bow, then return the gifts.'
translations.idiomatic: The Kunmo received Zhang Qian with the courtesy shown a Xiongnu chanyu, which mortified the envoy. Zhang Qian warned him, 'These are gifts from the Son of Heaven—if you will not bow in acceptance, we shall take them back.'

sentences.zh: 」昆莫起拜，其他如故。

translations.literal: The Kunmo rose and bowed; for the rest it was as before.
translations.idiomatic: The Kunmo rose and performed the bow; on other points he did not change his stance.

sentences.zh: 初，昆莫有十餘子，中子大祿強，善將，將眾萬餘騎別居。

translations.literal: At first the Kunmo had more than ten sons; the middle son Dalu was strong, good at leading, leading a host of more than ten thousand cavalry dwelling separately.
translations.idiomatic: The Kunmo had fathered more than a dozen sons; his middle son, the powerful Dalu, was a gifted commander who camped apart with more than ten thousand horsemen under his banner.

sentences.zh: 大祿兄太子，太子有子曰岑陬。

translations.literal: Dalu's elder brother was the heir apparent; the heir apparent had a son named Cenqu.
translations.idiomatic: Dalu's elder brother was the crown prince, whose own son was called Cenqu.

sentences.zh: 太子蚤死，謂昆莫曰：「必以岑陬為太子。

translations.literal: The heir apparent died early; he said to the Kunmo: 'You must make Cenqu heir apparent.'
translations.idiomatic: When the crown prince died young, he begged the Kunmo, 'You must name Cenqu as my successor.'

sentences.zh: 」昆莫哀許之。

translations.literal: The Kunmo in pity promised it.
translations.idiomatic: Moved by pity, the Kunmo agreed.

sentences.zh: 大祿怒，乃收其昆弟，將眾畔，謀攻岑陬。

translations.literal: Dalu was angry; thereupon he seized his younger brothers, led the host in revolt, and plotted to attack Cenqu.
translations.idiomatic: Dalu, furious, detained his junior kinsmen, mustered his riders in open defiance, and plotted to strike down Cenqu.

sentences.zh: 昆莫與芩陬萬餘騎，令別居，昆莫亦自有萬餘騎以自備。

translations.literal: The Kunmo with Cenqu more than ten thousand cavalry ordered separate dwelling; the Kunmo also himself had more than ten thousand cavalry for self-protection.
translations.idiomatic: The Kunmo gave Cenqu a separate command of over ten thousand horse and kept another ten thousand under his own hand as a personal guard.

sentences.zh: 國分為三，大總羈屬昆莫。

translations.literal: The state split into three; in the large overall halter-rein they belonged to the Kunmo.
translations.idiomatic: The realm fractured into three camps, yet all still nominally answered to the Kunmo.

sentences.zh: 騫既致賜，諭指曰：「烏孫能東居故地，則漢遣公主為夫人，結為昆弟，共距匈奴，不足破也。

translations.literal: When Qian had delivered the gifts, he instructed the intent, saying: 'If Wusun can dwell east in the old lands, then Han will send a princess as consort, bind as elder and younger brother, together resist the Xiongnu; they are not enough to be difficult to break.'
translations.idiomatic: After presenting the imperial gifts Zhang Qian explained Han's offer: 'Move back east to your ancient pastures, and the court will send a princess as your queen and treat you as a sworn brother; together we can shatter the Xiongnu—they are not invincible.'

sentences.zh: 」烏孫遠漢，未知其大小，又近匈奴，服屬日久，其大臣皆不欲徙。

translations.literal: Wusun is far from Han, does not know its size; moreover it is near the Xiongnu, submission and belonging long time; its great ministers all do not wish to move.
translations.idiomatic: Wusun lay far from Han and could not gauge the empire's strength; pressed close by the Xiongnu and long habituated to their overlordship, the great nobles refused to migrate.

sentences.zh: 昆莫年老國分，不能專制，乃發使送騫，因獻馬數十匹報謝。

translations.literal: The Kunmo was old and the state divided; he could not monopolize control; thereupon he dispatched envoys to escort Qian, and presented several tens of horses in return thanks.
translations.idiomatic: Aging and unable to command a divided court, the Kunmo sent Zhang Qian home under escort with a gift of several dozen horses by way of thanks.

sentences.zh: 其使見漢人眾富厚，歸其國，其國後乃益重漢。

translations.literal: Their envoys saw the Han people numerous and rich and thick; returning to their state, afterward their state then increasingly esteemed Han.
translations.idiomatic: Those envoys, dazzled by Han's wealth and population, carried home such reports that Wusun began to take the empire seriously.

sentences.zh: 匈奴聞其與漢通，怒欲擊之。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu heard that they communicated with Han; angry, they wished to strike them.
translations.idiomatic: When the Xiongnu learned that Wusun was courting Han, they threatened invasion.

sentences.zh: 又漢使烏孫，乃出其南，抵大宛、月氏，相屬不絕。

translations.literal: Moreover Han envoys to Wusun then went out their south, reaching Dayuan and Yuezhi, linked one after another without break.
translations.idiomatic: Han embassies to Wusun also opened a southern corridor through Dayuan and the Yuezhi so that missions marched in an unbroken chain.

sentences.zh: 烏孫於是恐，使使獻馬，願得尚漢公主，為昆弟。

translations.literal: Wusun thereupon was frightened; they sent envoys presenting horses, wished to obtain marrying a Han princess, to be elder and younger brother.
translations.idiomatic: Alarmed, Wusun sent tribute horses and asked to marry a Han princess in a sworn-brother alliance.

sentences.zh: 天子問群臣，議許，曰：「必先內聘，然後遣女。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven asked the host of ministers; deliberation permitted, saying: 'There must first be inner bride-price, afterward send the woman.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor polled his ministers, who approved the match on condition that 'the bride-price must arrive in the capital before we dispatch the princess.'

sentences.zh: 」烏孫以馬千匹聘。

translations.literal: Wusun with one thousand horses as bride-price.
translations.idiomatic: Wusun forwarded a thousand horses as the betrothal gift.

sentences.zh: 漢元封中，遣江都王建女細君為公主，以妻焉。

translations.literal: In Han's Yuanfeng period, dispatched Princess Xijun, daughter of Liu Jian king of Jiangdu, to wife him.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yuanfeng reign the court invested Liu Jian's daughter Xijun, princess of Jiangdu, as an imperial princess and sent her to be the Kunmo's consort.

sentences.zh: 賜乘輿服御物，為備官屬宦官侍御數百人，贈送甚盛。

translations.literal: Granted imperial carriage garments and use objects; for preparation official subordinates eunuchs attendants and several hundred persons; gifts sent very lavish.
translations.idiomatic: She traveled with imperial wardrobe pieces, a household staff of several hundred—including eunuch attendants—and a dowry train of staggering splendor.

sentences.zh: 烏孫昆莫以為右夫人。

translations.literal: The Wusun Kunmo made her right consort.
translations.idiomatic: The Kunmo installed her as senior consort on the right—the honored side in steppe protocol.

sentences.zh: 匈奴亦遣女妻昆莫，昆莫以為左夫人。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu also sent a woman to wife the Kunmo; the Kunmo made her left consort.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu countered by sending a bride of their own, whom the Kunmo ranked as left consort, the junior position.

sentences.zh: 公主至其國，自治宮室居，歲時一再與昆莫會，置酒飲食，以幣、帛賜王左右貴人。

translations.literal: The princess arrived at their state, herself managed palace chambers to dwell; year and season once or twice met with the Kunmo, set wine drink and food, with silks and cloths gifted the king's left and right nobles.
translations.idiomatic: Once in Wusun she built her own residence, met the Kunmo only once or twice a year over feasts, and used bolts of silk to court his chief nobles.

sentences.zh: 昆莫年老，言語不通，公主悲愁，自為作歌曰：「吾家嫁我兮天一方，遠托異國兮烏孫王。

translations.literal: The Kunmo was old; speech did not communicate; the princess was sad and melancholy; herself for herself made a song, saying: 'My family married me to a corner of heaven; far entrusted to a foreign state to the Wusun king.'
translations.idiomatic: The aged Kunmo spoke no Chinese, and the princess, heartsick, composed a lament: 'My kin have wed me to the rim of the sky, far off to serve a foreign king in Wusun.'

sentences.zh: 穹廬為室兮旃為牆，以肉為食兮酪為漿。

translations.literal: Felt yurt as chamber, felt as walls; taking meat as food, kumis as broth.
translations.idiomatic: Felt tents for halls and felt for walls, flesh for grain and fermented milk for wine.

sentences.zh: 居常土思兮心內傷，願為黃鵠兮歸故鄉。

translations.literal: Dwelling constantly homesick, heart within wounded; wish to be a yellow swan, return to old village.
translations.idiomatic: Homesickness ever gnaws my heart; would I were a yellow swan winging home.

sentences.zh: 」天子聞而憐之，間歲遣使者持帷帳錦繡給遺焉。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven heard and pitied her; alternate years dispatched envoys holding curtains and brocade silks to supply and gift her.
translations.idiomatic: Moved by the song, the emperor sent envoys every other year with canopies, hangings, and brocades to comfort her.

sentences.zh: 昆莫年老，欲使其孫岑陬尚公主。

translations.literal: The Kunmo was old; wished to make his grandson Cenqu marry the princess.
translations.idiomatic: In his old age the Kunmo proposed that his grandson Cenqu inherit the princess according to levirate custom.

sentences.zh: 公主不聽，上書言狀，天子報曰：「從其國俗，欲與烏孫共滅胡。

translations.literal: The princess did not listen; submitted a memorial stating the situation; the Son of Heaven replied saying: 'Follow their state's customs; wish with Wusun together to destroy the Hu.'
translations.idiomatic: She protested in a memorial to the throne, but the emperor answered, 'Bow to their customs if you would join Wusun in destroying the barbarians.'

sentences.zh: 」岑陬遂妻公主。

translations.literal: Cenqu thereupon took the princess as wife.
translations.idiomatic: Cenqu therefore married her in the Kunmo's stead.

sentences.zh: 昆莫死，岑陬代立。

translations.literal: The Kunmo died; Cenqu replaced and stood.
translations.idiomatic: When the Kunmo died, Cenqu succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 岑陬者，官號也，名軍須靡。

translations.literal: Cenqu is an official title; his name is Junxumi.
translations.idiomatic: Cenqu was a title, not a personal name; the man was Junxumi.

sentences.zh: 昆莫，王號也，名獵驕靡。

translations.literal: Kunmo is a king title; name Liejiaomi.
translations.idiomatic: Kunmo was the royal style; his given name was Liejiaomi.

sentences.zh: 後書「昆彌」雲。

translations.literal: Later documents write 'Kunmi,' it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Later writers regularized the title as Kunmi.

sentences.zh: 岑陬尚江都公主，生一女少夫。

translations.literal: Cenqu married the Jiangdu princess; bore one daughter Shaofu.
translations.idiomatic: Cenqu and the Jiangdu princess had a daughter named Shaofu.

sentences.zh: 公主死，漢復以楚王戊之孫解憂為公主，妻岑陬。

translations.literal: The princess died; Han again took Princess Jieyou, granddaughter of Prince Wu king of Chu, as princess, to wife Cenqu.
translations.idiomatic: After that princess died, the court invested Liu Wu's granddaughter Jieyou as princess and sent her to marry Cenqu.

sentences.zh: 岑陬胡婦子泥靡尚小，岑陬且死，以國與季父大祿子翁歸靡，曰：「泥靡大，以國歸之。」

translations.literal: Cenqu's Hu wife's son Nimi was still small; Cenqu about to die, gave the state to paternal uncle Dalu's son Wenguimi, saying: 'When Nimi is grown, return the state to him.'
translations.idiomatic: Cenqu's son Nimi by his Xiongnu wife was still a child. On his deathbed Cenqu handed the realm to his uncle Dalu's son Wenguimi, charging him, 'When Nimi comes of age, give him back the throne.'

sentences.zh: 翁歸靡既立，號肥王，復尚楚主解憂，生三男兩女：長男曰元貴靡；

translations.literal: Wenguimi having been established, styled Fat King, again married Chu princess Jieyou, bore three sons two daughters: eldest son called Yuanguimi;
translations.idiomatic: Wenguimi, enthroned as the Fat King, took Princess Jieyou of Chu and fathered three sons and two daughters; the eldest son was Yuanguimi;

sentences.zh: 次曰萬年，為莎車王；

translations.literal: Next called Wannian, became king of Suoju;
translations.idiomatic: the second, Wannian, became king of Shache (Yarkand);

sentences.zh: 次曰大樂，為左大將；

translations.literal: Next called Dale, was left grand general;
translations.idiomatic: the third, Dale, rose to command the left wing as grand general;

sentences.zh: 長女弟史為龜茲王絳賓妻；

translations.literal: Eldest daughter Dishi was wife of Qiuci king Jiangbin;
translations.idiomatic: the eldest daughter, Dishi, married Jiangbin, king of Kucha;

sentences.zh: 小女素光為若呼翕侯妻。

translations.literal: Youngest daughter Suguang was wife of Ruo-hu Xihou.
translations.idiomatic: the youngest, Suguang, wed a Ruo-hu noble holding the Xihou title.

sentences.zh: 昭帝時，公主上書，言：「匈奴發騎田車師，車師與匈奴為一，共侵烏孫，唯天子幸救之！

translations.literal: In Emperor Zhao's time, the princess submitted a memorial, saying: 'The Xiongnu dispatched cavalry to field-cultivate Jushi; Jushi and the Xiongnu are one, together invading Wusun; only the Son of Heaven deign save it!'
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Zhao the princess memorialized: 'Xiongnu horsemen are plundering Jushi, and that kingdom has thrown in its lot with them against us—only Your Majesty can rescue Wusun!'

sentences.zh: 」漢養士馬，議欲擊匈奴。

translations.literal: Han raised soldiers and horses; deliberated wishing to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Han began drilling troops and mounts for a punitive strike on the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 會昭帝崩，宣帝初即位，公主及昆彌皆遣使上書，言：「匈奴復連發大兵侵兵烏孫，取車延、惡師地，收人民去，使使謂烏孫趣持公主來，欲隔絕漢。

translations.literal: Meeting Emperor Zhao's collapse, when Emperor Xuan first took the throne, the princess and Kunmi both dispatched envoys submitting memorials, saying: 'The Xiongnu again repeatedly dispatched great armies to invade troops Wusun, took Cheyan and Eshi lands, gathered people and went away, sent envoys telling Wusun hasten bring the princess come, wishing to cut off Han.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhao died just then; as Emperor Xuan took the throne, both the princess and the Kunmi sent pleas: 'The Xiongnu have again hurled great armies against Wusun, seized the districts of Cheyan and Eshi, carried off our people, and demanded that we hand over the Han princess at once to sever Wusun from the empire.'

sentences.zh: 昆彌願發國半精兵，自給人馬五萬騎，盡力擊匈奴。

translations.literal: The Kunmi wishes to dispatch half the state's picked troops, self-supplying men and horses fifty thousand cavalry, exhaust strength to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The Kunmi offers to raise half the nation's best warriors—fifty thousand horse of his own provisioning—and throw them fully against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 唯天子出兵以救公主、昆彌。

translations.literal: Only the Son of Heaven dispatch troops to save the princess and Kunmi.
translations.idiomatic: We beg the Son of Heaven to march an army to rescue the princess and the Kunmi.

sentences.zh: 」漢兵大發十五萬騎，五將軍分道並出。

translations.literal: Han troops greatly dispatched one hundred fifty thousand cavalry, five generals by separate roads together went out.
translations.idiomatic: Han answered by mobilizing one hundred fifty thousand cavalry under five generals advancing on separate routes.

sentences.zh: 語在《匈奴傳》。

translations.literal: The words are in the 'Traditions of the Xiongnu.'
translations.idiomatic: The campaign is narrated in the treatise on the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 遣校尉常惠使持節護烏孫兵，昆彌自將翕侯以下五萬騎從西方人，至右谷蠡王庭，獲單于父行及嫂、居次、名王、犁水於都尉、千長、騎將以下四萬級，馬、牛、羊、驢、橐駝七十餘萬頭，烏孫皆自取所虜獲。

translations.literal: Sent Colonel Chang Hui as envoy bearing staff to protect Wusun troops; the Kunmi personally led from Xihou downward fifty thousand cavalry from the western men, reaching the Right Guli Prince's court, captured the Chanyu's father's generation and sister-in-law, Juluci, famous kings, plow-water Yuduwei, thousand-chiefs, cavalry generals downward forty thousand grades, horses, cattle, sheep, donkeys, camels more than seven hundred thousand head; Wusun all themselves took what they captured.
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Chang Hui went with imperial credentials to coordinate the Wusun host; the Kunmi personally led fifty thousand horsemen of his western nobles to the court of the Right Guli Prince, cutting off forty thousand ranked heads from the chanyu's kinsmen, consorts, princesses, great kings, commandants, chiliarchs, and cavalry commanders, and driving off more than seven hundred thousand head of livestock—all of which the Wusun kept as their own spoils.

sentences.zh: 還，封惠為長羅侯。

translations.literal: Returning, enfeoffed Hui as marquis of Changluo.
translations.idiomatic: On his return Chang Hui was enfeoffed as marquis of Changluo.

sentences.zh: 是歲，本始三年也。

translations.literal: This year was Benshi third year.
translations.idiomatic: The year was the third of the Benshi era.

sentences.zh: 漢遣惠持金幣賜烏孫貴人有功者。

translations.literal: Han dispatched Hui bearing gold and silks to gift Wusun nobles who had merit.
translations.idiomatic: The court then sent Chang Hui back with gold and silk to reward the Wusun nobles who had distinguished themselves.

sentences.zh: 元康二年，烏孫昆彌因惠上書：「願以漢外孫元貴靡為嗣，得令復尚漢公主，結婚重親，畔絕匈奴，原聘馬、騾各千匹。

translations.literal: In Yuankang second year, the Wusun Kunmi through Hui submitted a memorial: 'Wish to take Han's maternal grandson Yuanguimi as heir, obtain order again to marry Han princess, bind marriage double kinship, revolt cut off Xiongnu, wish bride-price horses and mules each one thousand head.'
translations.idiomatic: In the second year of Yuankang the Kunmi petitioned through Chang Hui: 'Let my Han-born grandson Yuanguimi succeed me, marry him to another imperial princess to renew the double marriage, and break with the Xiongnu—we will offer one thousand horses and one thousand mules as bride-price.'

sentences.zh: 」詔下公卿議，大鴻臚蕭望之以為：「烏孫絕域，變故難保，不可許。

translations.literal: Edict sent down to dukes and ministers to deliberate; Grand Herald Xiao Wangzhi thought: 'Wusun is cut-off territory; accidents and changes hard to guarantee; cannot permit.'
translations.idiomatic: The edict went to the high ministers for debate; Grand Herald Xiao Wangzhi argued that Wusun lay beyond reliable reach and that turmoil there could not be controlled—best to refuse.

sentences.zh: 」上美烏孫新立大功，又重絕故業，遣使者至烏孫，先迎取聘。

translations.literal: The sovereign prized Wusun's newly established great merit, again heavily valued cutting off the old enterprise, dispatched envoys to Wusun, first to welcome and take the bride-price.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, honoring Wusun's fresh victory and reluctant to abandon a proven policy, sent envoys ahead to Wusun to accept the bride-price anyway.

sentences.zh: 昆彌及太子、左右大將、都尉皆遣使，凡三百餘人，入漢迎取少主。

translations.literal: The Kunmi and heir apparent, left and right grand generals, commandants all dispatched envoys, in all more than three hundred persons, entered Han to welcome and fetch the young mistress.
translations.idiomatic: The Kunmi, crown prince, senior generals, and commandants dispatched over three hundred men into Han to escort the young princess west.

sentences.zh: 上乃以烏孫主解憂弟子相夫為公主，置官屬侍御百餘人，捨上林中，學烏孫言。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon took Wusun ruler Jieyou's disciple niece Xiangfu as princess, set up official subordinates attendants more than one hundred persons, lodged in Shanglin, studied Wusun speech.
translations.idiomatic: He invested Jieyou's niece Xiangfu as princess, gave her a household of more than a hundred attendants, quartered her in the Shanglin Park, and had her drilled in the Wusun tongue.

sentences.zh: 天子自臨平樂觀，會匈奴使者、外國君長大角抵，設樂而遣之。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven personally attended Pingleguan, assembled Xiongnu envoys, foreign rulers and chiefs, great horn-butting, set music and dispatched them.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor himself appeared at the Pingle Lodge to stage a grand acrobatic show for the Xiongnu envoys and foreign dignitaries before sending the bridal party off with music.

sentences.zh: 使長羅侯光祿大夫惠為副，凡持節者四人，送少主至郭煌。

translations.literal: Made Marquis of Changluo Imperial Household Grandee Hui as deputy; in all staff-bearers four persons, escorted the young mistress to Guohuang.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Changluo, now Grandee of the Imperial Household Chang Hui, served as deputy to four credential-bearing envoys who convoyed the young princess as far as Dunhuang (here written Guohuang).

sentences.zh: 未出塞，聞烏孫昆彌翁歸靡死，烏孫貴人共從本約，立岑陬子泥靡代為昆靡，號狂王。

translations.literal: Not yet having gone out beyond the pass, heard Wusun Kunmi Wenguimi died; Wusun nobles together followed the original covenant, established Cenqu's son Nimi to replace as Kunmi, styled Mad King.
translations.idiomatic: Before she crossed the frontier, word arrived that Kunmi Wenguimi was dead; the Wusun nobles, invoking the old pact, enthroned Cenqu's son Nimi as Kunmi with the sobriquet Mad King.

sentences.zh: 惠上書：「願留少主郭煌，惠馳至烏孫責讓不立元貴靡為昆靡，還迎少主。

translations.literal: Hui submitted a memorial: 'Wish to detain the young mistress at Guohuang; Hui gallop to Wusun rebuke for not establishing Yuanguimi as Kunmi, return and welcome the young mistress.'
translations.idiomatic: Chang Hui memorialized: 'Hold the young princess at Dunhuang while I ride post-haste to rebuke Wusun for ignoring Yuanguimi's claim, then bring her home if they persist in error.'

sentences.zh: 」事下公卿，望之復以為：「烏孫持兩端，難約結。

translations.literal: The matter sent down to dukes and ministers; Wangzhi again thought: 'Wusun holds two ends; hard to bind covenant.'
translations.idiomatic: The case went back to the ministers; Xiao Wangzhi repeated that Wusun played both sides and could not be bound by treaty.

sentences.zh: 前公主在烏孫四十餘年，恩愛不親密，邊竟未得安，此已事已驗也。

translations.literal: Former princess in Wusun forty-some years, favor love not intimate; border region not obtained peace; this past matter already verified.
translations.idiomatic: The last princess spent forty years there without winning real affection or quieting the frontier—that experiment has already failed.

sentences.zh: 令少主以元貴靡不立而還，信無負於夷狄，中國之福也。

translations.literal: Order the young mistress because Yuanguimi not established to return; truly without fault toward the Yi-Di; Central States' blessing.
translations.idiomatic: Recalling the girl because Yuanguimi was passed over costs us nothing in the eyes of the barbarians and is a blessing for the Middle Kingdom.

sentences.zh: 少主不止，徭役將興，其原起此。

translations.literal: If the young mistress does not stop, corvée labor will arise; its origin begins here.
translations.idiomatic: If she pushes on, corvée and war will follow—and the trouble will have started here.

sentences.zh: 」天子從之，征還少主。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven followed it, summoned back the young mistress.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed and recalled the young princess.

sentences.zh: 狂王復尚楚主解憂，生一男鴟靡，不與主和，又暴惡失眾。

translations.literal: The Mad King again married Chu princess Jieyou, bore one son Chimi, did not harmonize with the consort, again violent evil lost the host.
translations.idiomatic: The Mad King again married Princess Jieyou of Chu and fathered a son, Chimi, but he quarreled with her and ruled so brutally that he forfeited popular support.

sentences.zh: 漢使衛司馬魏和意、副侯任昌送侍子，公主言狂王為烏孫所患苦，易誅也。

translations.literal: Han envoys Guard Colonel Wei Heyi, Deputy Marquis Ren Chang escorted the hostage prince; the princess said the Mad King was what Wusun suffered from, easy to execute.
translations.idiomatic: When Guard Colonel Wei Heyi and Deputy Marquis Ren Chang arrived to escort the hostage heir, the princess confided that the Mad King tormented the realm and could easily be eliminated.

sentences.zh: 遂謀置酒會，罷，使士拔劍擊之。

translations.literal: Thereupon plotted to set a wine banquet; when finished, ordered soldiers draw swords and strike him.
translations.idiomatic: They plotted a banquet, and when the cups were cleared signaled their guards to draw swords and strike him down.

sentences.zh: 劍旁下，狂王傷，上馬馳去。

translations.literal: The sword went sideways down; the Mad King was wounded, mounted horse and galloped away.
translations.idiomatic: The blade glanced aside; wounded, the Mad King vaulted onto a horse and fled.

sentences.zh: 其子細沈瘦會兵圍和意、昌及公主於赤谷城。

translations.literal: His son Xishenshou assembled troops and surrounded Heyi, Chang, and the princess at Chigu city.
translations.idiomatic: His son Xishenshou raised troops and besieged Wei Heyi, Ren Chang, and the princess in Chigu.

sentences.zh: 數月，都護鄭吉發諸國兵救之，乃解去。

translations.literal: Several months; Protector-General Zheng Ji raised various states' troops to save them; then they dispersed and left.
translations.idiomatic: For months they remained trapped until Protector-General Zheng Ji mobilized the oasis armies and lifted the siege.

sentences.zh: 漢遣中郎將張遵持醫藥治狂王，賜金二十斤，采繒。

translations.literal: Han dispatched Palace Gentleman Zhang Zun bearing medicine to treat the Mad King, gifted gold twenty jin, colored silks.
translations.idiomatic: Han sent Gentleman Zhang Zun with physicians and drugs to heal the Mad King and showered him with twenty jin of gold and brocades.

sentences.zh: 因收和意、昌系鎖，從尉犁檻車至長安，斬之。

translations.literal: Thereupon seized Heyi and Chang, bound with fetters, from Yanqi in caged carts to Chang'an, executed them.
translations.idiomatic: At the same time Wei Heyi and Ren Chang were shackled, hauled in prison carts from Yanqi to Chang'an, and beheaded.

sentences.zh: 車騎將軍長史張翁留驗公主與使者謀殺狂王狀，主不服，叩頭謝，張翁捽主頭罵詈。

translations.literal: Chariots and Cavalry General senior clerk Zhang Weng stayed to examine the princess with envoys plotting to kill the Mad King situation; the mistress did not submit, knocked head in apology; Zhang Weng seized the mistress's head and reviled.
translations.idiomatic: Chief clerk Zhang Weng of the chariot command stayed to interrogate the princess on the plot to murder the Mad King; when she denied any part in it he forced her brow to the floor, seized her by the hair, and showered abuse on her.

sentences.zh: 主上書，翁還，坐死。

translations.literal: The mistress submitted a memorial; Weng returned, sat crime death.
translations.idiomatic: She appealed to the throne; Zhang Weng, once back at court, was convicted and executed.

sentences.zh: 副使季都別將醫養視狂王，狂王從十餘騎送之。

translations.literal: Deputy envoy Ji Du separately led physicians to nurture and look at the Mad King; the Mad King followed more than ten cavalry escorting him.
translations.idiomatic: Deputy envoy Ji Du detached a medical party to tend the Mad King, who saw them off with a dozen horsemen.

sentences.zh: 都還，坐知狂王當誅，見便不發，下蠶室。

translations.literal: Du returned; sat for knowing the Mad King ought to be executed, seeing convenience not acting; sent down silkworm chamber.
translations.idiomatic: When Ji Du returned he was judged for knowing the Mad King deserved execution yet failing to strike when he had the chance, and was castrated.

sentences.zh: 初，肥王翁歸靡胡婦子烏就屠，狂五傷時驚，與諸翕侯俱去，居北山中，揚言母家匈奴兵來，故眾歸之。

translations.literal: At first Fat King Wenguimi's Hu wife's son Wujiutu, when Kuang-wu was wounded then frightened, together with various Xihou all left, dwelt in north mountains, loudly said mother's family Xiongnu troops come, therefore the host returned to him.
translations.idiomatic: Wujiutu, son of the Fat King by his Xiongnu consort, fled in panic when the Mad King was wounded, withdrew into the northern hills with several Xihou, and spread word that Xiongnu reinforcements from his mother's kin were coming—so the tribes flocked to him.

sentences.zh: 後遂襲殺狂王，自立為昆彌。

translations.literal: Afterward thereupon raided and killed the Mad King, himself established as Kunmi.
translations.idiomatic: He then ambushed and killed the Mad King and proclaimed himself Kunmi.

sentences.zh: 漢遣破羌將軍辛武賢將兵萬五千人至郭煌，遣使者案行表，穿卑鞮侯井以西，欲通渠轉谷，積居廬倉以討之。

translations.literal: Han dispatched Qiang-breaker General Xin Wuxian leading troops fifteen thousand men to Guohuang, dispatched envoys to survey and mark the route, dig Beiji-hou wells westward, wished to open canals transport grain, pile garrison granaries to strike them.
translations.idiomatic: Han sent General Xin Wuxian, the Qiang-breaker, to Dunhuang with fifteen thousand men, ordered survey parties to stake out a line of march, sink Beiji-hou wells west of the frontier, cut canals to float grain forward, and stockpile supplies in border depots for the coming campaign.

sentences.zh: 初，楚主侍者馮嫽能史書，習事，嘗持漢書為公主使，行賞賜於城郭諸國，敬信之，號曰馮夫人。

translations.literal: At first, the Chu princess's attendant Feng Liao was able in clerkly writing, practiced affairs, once held Han documents as the princess's envoy, went performing rewards and gifts among the walled-city states; they revered and trusted her, styled her Lady Feng.
translations.idiomatic: Among the princess of Chu's maids was Feng Liao, literate and experienced, who had often carried Han credentials on her mistress's behalf to distribute gifts among the oasis kingdoms; they honored her and called her Lady Feng.

sentences.zh: 為烏孫右大將妻，右大將與烏就屠相愛，都護鄭吉使馮夫人說烏就屠，以漢兵方出，必見滅，不如降。

translations.literal: She was wife of Wusun's right grand general; the right grand general and Wujiutu loved each other; Protector-General Zheng Ji sent Lady Feng to persuade Wujiutu, that Han troops were just coming out, would surely see destruction, not as good as surrender.
translations.idiomatic: She had married Wusun's right grand general, who was close to Wujiutu; Protector-General Zheng Ji therefore sent Lady Feng to warn Wujiutu that Han armies were on the march and resistance meant annihilation, while submission was the safer course.

sentences.zh: 烏就屠恐，曰：「願得小號。

translations.literal: Wujiutu was frightened, saying: 'Wish to obtain a small title.'
translations.idiomatic: Wujiutu, alarmed, said, 'I will accept a lesser title.'

sentences.zh: 」宣帝征馮夫人，自問狀。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan summoned Lady Feng, himself asked the situation.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan summoned Lady Feng to the capital and questioned her in person.

sentences.zh: 遣謁者竺次、期門甘延壽為副，送馮夫人。

translations.literal: Dispatched yezhu Zhu Ci, palace guard Gan Yanshou as deputies, to escort Lady Feng.
translations.idiomatic: He sent the herald Zhu Ci and the palace guard Gan Yanshou as her escorts to see her off.

sentences.zh: 馮夫人錦車持節，詔烏就屠詣長羅侯赤谷城，立元貴靡為大昆彌，烏就屠為小昆彌，皆賜印綬。

translations.literal: Lady Feng in brocade carriage bearing staff, edict Wujiutu go to Marquis of Changluo at Chigu city, established Yuanguimi as great Kunmi, Wujiutu as small Kunmi, all gifted seals and cords.
translations.idiomatic: Riding in a brocade-hung carriage and bearing the imperial staff, Lady Feng ordered Wujiutu to meet Marquis Changluo at Chigu, where Yuanguimi was recognized as senior Kunmi and Wujiutu as junior Kunmi, each receiving seal and ribbon.

sentences.zh: 破羌將軍不出塞還。

translations.literal: The Qiang-breaker general did not go out beyond the pass and returned.
translations.idiomatic: General Xin Wuxian turned back without crossing the frontier.

sentences.zh: 後烏就屠不盡歸諸翕侯民眾，漢復遣長羅侯惠將三校屯赤谷，因為分別其人民地界，大昆彌戶六萬餘，小昆彌戶四萬餘，然眾心皆附小昆彌。

translations.literal: Afterward Wujiutu did not fully return the various Xihou's people masses; Han again dispatched Marquis of Changluo Hui leading three colonels to garrison Chigu, thereby for them dividing their people and land boundaries; great Kunmi households more than sixty thousand, small Kunmi households more than forty thousand, yet the host's hearts all attached to the small Kunmi.
translations.idiomatic: When Wujiutu refused to hand back all the tribesmen of the Xihou nobles, Han sent Marquis Changluo Chang Hui with three garrison commands to hold Chigu and partition subjects and territory: the senior line counted more than sixty thousand households and the junior more than forty thousand, yet popular loyalty clung to the junior Kunmi.

sentences.zh: 元貴靡、鴟靡皆病死，公主上書言年老土思，願得歸骸骨，葬漢地。

translations.literal: Yuanguimi and Chimi both died of illness; the princess submitted a memorial saying old age homesickness, wished to obtain return bones, bury in Han land.
translations.idiomatic: After Yuanguimi and Chimi both died of illness, the princess memorialized that age and longing for home moved her to ask leave to end her days in Han soil.

sentences.zh: 天子閔而迎之，公主與烏孫男女三人俱來至京師。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven pitied and welcomed her; the princess together with three Wusun male and female persons all came to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor took pity and summoned her home; the princess arrived at the capital with three of her Wusun children.

sentences.zh: 是歲，甘露三年也。

translations.literal: This year was Ganlu third year.
translations.idiomatic: The year was the third of the Ganlu era.

sentences.zh: 時年且七十，賜以公主田宅、奴婢，奉養甚厚，朝見儀比公主。

translations.literal: At the time her age was about seventy; granted her princess fields, houses, slaves, maids; support and nurture very thick; court audience rites compared to a princess.
translations.idiomatic: She was nearly seventy; the court granted her an estate, servants, and a generous stipend, and received her at audience with the ceremony due an imperial princess.

sentences.zh: 後二歲卒，三孫因留守墳墓雲。

translations.literal: After two years died; three grandsons therefore stayed guarding tombs, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: She died two years later, while three grandsons remained in Wusun to tend the family graves, it is said.

sentences.zh: 元貴靡子星靡代為大昆彌，弱，馮夫人上書，願使烏孫鎮撫星靡。

translations.literal: Yuanguimi's son Xingmi replaced as great Kunmi, weak; Lady Feng submitted a memorial, wished to be sent to Wusun to pacify and soothe Xingmi.
translations.idiomatic: Yuanguimi's son Xingmi succeeded as senior Kunmi while still a boy; Lady Feng asked to return to Wusun to steady the young ruler.

sentences.zh: 漢遣之，卒百人送焉。

translations.literal: Han dispatched her; one hundred soldiers escorted her.
translations.idiomatic: The court approved and sent a hundred troops to escort her west.

sentences.zh: 都護韓宣奏，烏孫大吏、大祿、大監皆可以賜金印紫綬，以尊輔大昆彌，漢許之。

translations.literal: Protector-General Han Xuan memorialized: Wusun grand clerk, Dalu, grand supervisor all can be granted gold seal purple cord, thereby to honor and assist the great Kunmi; Han permitted it.
translations.idiomatic: Protector-General Han Xuan proposed investing Wusun's chief clerk, grand steward, and chief overseer with gold seals and purple ribbons to bolster the senior Kunmi; the court agreed.

sentences.zh: 後都護韓宣復奏，星靡怯弱，可免，更以季父左大將樂代為昆彌，漢不許。

translations.literal: Afterward Protector-General Han Xuan again memorialized: Xingmi timid weak, can be removed, change to paternal uncle left grand general Yue replace as Kunmi; Han did not permit.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xuan later urged that the timid Xingmi be set aside in favor of his uncle, the left grand general Yue; the court refused.

sentences.zh: 後段會宗為都護，招還亡畔，安定之。

translations.literal: Afterward Duan Huizong became Protector-General, summoned back fugitives and rebels, pacified it.
translations.idiomatic: When Duan Huizong took office as Protector-General he recalled deserters and rebels and restored order.

sentences.zh: 星靡死，子雌栗靡代。

translations.literal: Xingmi died; son Cilimi replaced.
translations.idiomatic: Xingmi died and was succeeded by his son Cilimi.

sentences.zh: 小昆彌烏就屠死。

translations.literal: Small Kunmi Wujiutu died.
translations.idiomatic: The junior Kunmi Wujiutu died.

sentences.zh: 子拊離代立，為弟日貳所殺。

translations.literal: Son Fuli replaced and stood; was by younger brother Rier killed.
translations.idiomatic: His son Fuli succeeded but was murdered by his younger brother Rier.

sentences.zh: 漢遣使者立拊離子安日為小昆彌。

translations.literal: Han dispatched an envoy to establish Fuli's son Anri as small Kunmi.
translations.idiomatic: Han sent an envoy to enthrone Fuli's son Anri as junior Kunmi.

sentences.zh: 日貳亡，阻康居。

translations.literal: Rier fled; halted at Kangju.
translations.idiomatic: Rier fled and took refuge in Kangju.

sentences.zh: 漢徙已校屯姑墨，欲候便討焉。

translations.literal: Han moved the Yi colonel to garrison Gumo, wished to wait for convenience to strike there.
translations.idiomatic: Han shifted the Yi garrison colonel to Gumo to watch for a chance to strike him.

sentences.zh: 安日使貴人姑莫匿等三人詐亡從日貳，刺殺之。

translations.literal: Anri ordered nobles Gumoni and others three persons feign flight to follow Rier, stabbed and killed him.
translations.idiomatic: Anri sent three nobles, including Gumoni, to feign defection, join Rier, and assassinate him.

sentences.zh: 都護廉褒賜姑莫匿等金人二十斤，繒三百匹。

translations.literal: Protector-General Lian Bao gifted Gumoni and others gold persons twenty jin, silks three hundred bolts.
translations.idiomatic: Protector-General Lian Bao rewarded Gumoni's party with twenty jin of gold and three hundred bolts of silk.

sentences.zh: 後安日為降民所殺，漢立其弟末振將代。

translations.literal: Afterward Anri was by surrendered people killed; Han established his younger brother Mozhenjiang to replace.
translations.idiomatic: Later Anri was killed by surrendered tribesmen, and Han enthroned his brother Mozhenjiang in his place.

sentences.zh: 時大昆彌雌栗靡健，翕侯皆畏服之，告民牧馬畜無使人牧，國中大安和翁歸靡時。

translations.literal: At the time the great Kunmi Cilimi was vigorous; Xihou all feared and submitted to him; ordered the people herd horses livestock without letting others herd; within the state greatly peaceful as under Wenguimi's time.
translations.idiomatic: The senior Kunmi Cilimi was vigorous, and the Xihou nobles feared him; he forbade outsiders from pasturing herds in his domain, and the realm knew a calm like the days of Wenguimi.

sentences.zh: 小昆彌末振將恐為所並，使貴人烏日領詐降刺殺雌栗靡。

translations.literal: Small Kunmi Mozhenjiang feared being by him annexed; sent noble Wuriling feign surrender to stab and kill Cilimi.
translations.idiomatic: The junior Kunmi Mozhenjiang, fearing annexation, sent the noble Wuriling under false colors to murder Cilimi.

sentences.zh: 漢欲以兵討之而未能，遣中郎將段會宗持金幣與都護圖方略，立雌栗靡季父公主孫伊秩靡為大昆彌。

translations.literal: Han wished with troops to punish him but could not; dispatched Palace Gentleman Duan Huizong bearing gold and silks with the Protector-General to plan strategy, established Cilimi's paternal uncle the princess's grandson Yizhimi as great Kunmi.
translations.idiomatic: Han could not yet mount a punitive expedition, so it sent Gentleman Duan Huizong with treasure to concert plans with the Protector-General and set up Cilimi's uncle, the princess's grandson Yizhimi, as senior Kunmi.

sentences.zh: 漢沒入小昆彌侍子在京師者。

translations.literal: Han confiscated the small Kunmi's hostage sons who were at the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Han seized the junior Kunmi's hostages who were lodged in the capital.

sentences.zh: 久之，大昆彌翕侯難棲殺末振將，末振將兄安日子安犁靡代為小昆彌。

translations.literal: After a long time, great Kunmi Xihou Nanxi killed Mozhenjiang; Mozhenjiang's elder brother Anri's son Anlimi replaced as small Kunmi.
translations.idiomatic: Eventually the senior Kunmi's noble Nanxi killed Mozhenjiang, and Anri's grandson Anlimi succeeded as junior Kunmi.

sentences.zh: 漢恨不自誅末振將，復使段會宗即斬其太子番丘。

translations.literal: Han regretted not personally executing Mozhenjiang; again sent Duan Huizong immediately to behead his heir apparent Fanqiu.
translations.idiomatic: Angry that Mozhenjiang had not died by Han hands, the court ordered Duan Huizong to execute his crown prince Fanqiu on the spot.

sentences.zh: 還，賜爵關內侯。

translations.literal: Returning, gifted rank marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: On his return he was ennobled as a marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 是歲，元延二年也。

translations.literal: This year was Yuanyan second year.
translations.idiomatic: The year was the second of the Yuanyan era.

sentences.zh: 會宗以翕侯難棲殺末振將，雖不指為漢，合於討賊，奏以為堅守都尉。

translations.literal: Huizong, because Xihou Nanxi killed Mozhenjiang, although did not point to acting for Han, accorded with punishing rebels, memorialized to make him firm-garrison commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Duan Huizong argued that although Nanxi had not acted explicitly for Han, killing Mozhenjiang served justice against a traitor, and recommended him for appointment as commandant of the firm garrison.

sentences.zh: 責大祿、大吏、大監以雌栗靡見殺狀，奪金印紫綬，更與銅墨雲。

translations.literal: Reproached Dalu, grand clerk, grand supervisor with regard to Cilimi being killed situation; stripped gold seal purple cord; changed and gave bronze and black, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: He censured the grand steward, chief clerk, and chief overseer for allowing Cilimi's murder, stripped their gold seals and purple ribbons, and reassigned them seals of bronze with black cords.

sentences.zh: 末振將弟卑爰疐本共謀殺大昆彌，將眾八萬餘口北附康居，謀欲借兵兼併兩昆彌。

translations.literal: Mozhenjiang's younger brother Bei'aidi originally together plotted to kill the great Kunmi; led a host of more than eighty thousand mouths north attaching to Kangju; plotted wishing to borrow troops to annex both Kunmi.
translations.idiomatic: Mozhenjiang's brother Bei'aidi, who had conspired in the regicide, led more than eighty thousand followers north to Kangju and schemed to borrow troops to swallow both Kunmi lines.

sentences.zh: 兩昆彌畏之，親倚都護。

translations.literal: Both Kunmi feared him; personally leaned on the Protector-General.
translations.idiomatic: Both Kunmi feared him and clung to the Protector-General for protection.

sentences.zh: 哀帝元壽二年，大昆彌伊秩靡與單于並入朝，漢以為榮。

translations.literal: Emperor Ai's Yuanshou second year, great Kunmi Yizhimi together with the Chanyu both entered court; Han took it as glory.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year of Yuanshou under Emperor Ai the senior Kunmi Yizhimi entered the capital together with the chanyu—an occasion Han counted among its honors.

sentences.zh: 至元始中，卑爰疐殺烏日領以自效，漢封為歸義侯。

translations.literal: Reaching Yuanshi period, Bei'aidi killed Wuriling to show self-effort; Han enfeoffed him as marquis of Returning Righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yuanshi era Bei'aidi slew Wuriling to prove his loyalty and was enfeoffed as marquis of Returning Allegiance.

sentences.zh: 兩昆彌皆弱，卑爰疐侵陵，都護孫建襲殺之。

translations.literal: Both Kunmi were all weak; Bei'aidi encroached and bullied; Protector-General Sun Jian raided and killed him.
translations.idiomatic: With both Kunmi enfeebled, Bei'aidi bullied them until Protector-General Sun Jian ambushed and killed him.

sentences.zh: 自烏孫分立兩昆彌後，漢用憂勞，且無寧歲。

translations.literal: From Wusun splitting and establishing two Kunmi afterward, Han thereby worried and toiled, moreover without quiet years.
translations.idiomatic: From the day Wusun split into two Kunmi lines, Han was kept in constant anxiety and scarcely knew a year of peace.

sentences.zh: 姑墨國，王治南城，去長安八千一百五十里。

translations.literal: The state of Gumo: the king rules from South city; it is eight thousand one hundred fifty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Gumo was ruled from its southern capital, eight thousand one hundred fifty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶二千二百，口二萬四千五百，勝兵四千五百人。

translations.literal: Households two thousand two hundred; population twenty-four thousand five hundred; able soldiers four thousand five hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: It had twenty-two hundred households, twenty-four thousand five hundred inhabitants, and forty-five hundred men under arms.

sentences.zh: 姑墨侯、輔國侯、都尉、左右將、左右騎君各一人，譯長二人。

translations.literal: Gumo marquis, state-assisting marquis, commandant, left and right generals, left and right cavalry lords one each; interpreter chiefs two persons.
translations.idiomatic: Its officials included a Gumo marquis, an assistant-to-the-state marquis, a commandant, generals of the left and right, cavalry lords of the left and right—one of each—and two chiefs of interpreters.

sentences.zh: 東至都護治所二千二十一里，南至於闐馬行十五日，北與烏孫接。

translations.literal: East to the Protector-General's seat two thousand twenty-one li; south to Khotan horse travel fifteen days; north joins Wusun.
translations.idiomatic: It lay two thousand twenty-one li east of the Protector-General's headquarters, fifteen days' ride south to Khotan, and bordered Wusun on the north.

sentences.zh: 出銅、鐵、雌黃。

translations.literal: Produces copper, iron, orpiment.
translations.idiomatic: It produced copper, iron, and orpiment.

sentences.zh: 東通龜茲六百七十里。

translations.literal: East connects to Qiuci six hundred seventy li.
translations.idiomatic: Six hundred seventy li to the east lay Kucha.

sentences.zh: 王莽時，姑墨王丞殺溫宿王，並其國。

translations.literal: In Wang Mang's time, Gumo king Cheng killed the Wensu king and annexed its state.
translations.idiomatic: Under Wang Mang the king of Gumo, Cheng, slew the king of Wensu and annexed his realm.

sentences.zh: 溫宿國，王治溫宿城，去長安八千三百五十里，戶二千二百，口八千四百，勝兵千五百人。

translations.literal: The state of Wensu: the king rules from Wensu city; it is eight thousand three hundred fifty li from Chang'an; households two thousand two hundred; population eight thousand four hundred; able soldiers one thousand five hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: Wensu was ruled from Wensu city, eight thousand three hundred fifty li from Chang'an, with twenty-two hundred households, eighty-four hundred people, and fifteen hundred soldiers.

sentences.zh: 輔國侯、左右將、左右都尉、左右騎君、譯長各二人。

translations.literal: State-assisting marquis, left and right generals, left and right commandants, left and right cavalry lords, interpreter chiefs two each.
translations.idiomatic: It had two each of assistant marquises, left and right generals, left and right commandants, left and right cavalry lords, and interpreter chiefs.

sentences.zh: 東至都護治所二千三百八十里，西至尉頭三百里，北至烏孫赤谷六百一十里。

translations.literal: East to the Protector-General's seat two thousand three hundred eighty li; west to Weitou three hundred li; north to Wusun Chigu six hundred ten li.
translations.idiomatic: It stood twenty-three hundred eighty li east of the Protector-General, three hundred li west of Weitou, and six hundred ten li south of Wusun's Chigu.

sentences.zh: 土地物類所有與鄯善諸國同。

translations.literal: Land soil thing kinds possessed together with Shanshan various states same.
translations.idiomatic: Its terrain and products resembled those of Shanshan and the neighboring states.

sentences.zh: 東通姑墨二百七十里。

translations.literal: East connects to Gumo two hundred seventy li.
translations.idiomatic: Gumo lay two hundred seventy li to the east.

sentences.zh: 龜茲國，王治延城，去長安七千四百八十里。

translations.literal: The state of Qiuci: the king rules from Yan city; it is seven thousand four hundred eighty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Kucha was governed from Yan city, seven thousand four hundred eighty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶六千九百七十，口八萬一千三百一十七，勝兵二萬一千七十六人。

translations.literal: Households six thousand nine hundred seventy; population eighty-one thousand three hundred seventeen; able soldiers twenty-one thousand seventy-six persons.
translations.idiomatic: It counted six thousand nine hundred seventy households, eighty-one thousand three hundred seventeen people, and twenty-one thousand seventy-six troops.

sentences.zh: 大都尉丞、輔國侯、安國侯、擊胡侯、卻胡都尉、擊車師都尉、左右將、左右都尉、左右騎君、左右力輔君各一人，東西南北部千長各二人，卻胡君三人，譯長四人。

translations.literal: Grand commandant assistant, state-assisting marquis, state-securing marquis, strike-Hu marquis, repel-Hu commandant, strike-Jushi commandant, left and right generals, left and right commandants, left and right cavalry lords, left and right strength-assisting lords one each; east west south north department thousand-chiefs two each; repel-Hu lords three persons; interpreter chiefs four persons.
translations.idiomatic: Its bureaucracy included a grand commandant's aide, marquises for assisting and securing the state, marquises for striking and repelling the Hu, commandants for operations against the Hu and Jushi, paired generals, commandants, cavalry lords, and strength-assisting lords, two chiliarchs for each quarter of the realm, three Hu-repelling lords, and four interpreter chiefs.

sentences.zh: 南與精絕、東南與且末、西南與杅彌、北與烏孫、西與姑墨接。

translations.literal: South joins Jingjue; southeast joins Qiemo; southwest joins Wumi; north joins Wusun; west joins Gumo.
translations.idiomatic: It bordered Jingjue on the south, Qiemo to the southeast, Wumi to the southwest, Wusun to the north, and Gumo on the west.

sentences.zh: 能鑄冶，有鉛。

translations.literal: Able to cast and smelt; has lead.
translations.idiomatic: The people worked metal and had deposits of lead.

sentences.zh: 東至都護治所烏壘城三百五十里。

translations.literal: East to the Protector-General's governing place Wulei city three hundred fifty li.
translations.idiomatic: The Protector-General's seat at Wulei lay three hundred fifty li to the east.

sentences.zh: 烏壘，戶百一十，口千二百，勝兵三百人。

translations.literal: Wulei: households one hundred ten; population one thousand two hundred; able soldiers three hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: Wulei had one hundred ten households, twelve hundred inhabitants, and three hundred soldiers.

sentences.zh: 城都尉、譯長各一人。

translations.literal: City commandant, interpreter chief one each.
translations.idiomatic: It had one city commandant and one interpreter chief.

sentences.zh: 與都護同治。

translations.literal: Together with the Protector-General same rule.
translations.idiomatic: It shared the seat with the Protector-General.

sentences.zh: 其南三百三十里至渠犁。

translations.literal: Its south three hundred thirty li reaches Quli.
translations.idiomatic: Quli lay three hundred thirty li to the south.

sentences.zh: 渠梨，城都尉一人，戶百三十，口千四百八十，勝兵百五十人。

translations.literal: Quli: city commandant one person; households one hundred thirty; population one thousand four hundred eighty; able soldiers one hundred fifty persons.
translations.idiomatic: Quli was administered by a city commandant and counted one hundred thirty households, fourteen hundred eighty people, and one hundred fifty troops.

sentences.zh: 東北與尉犁、東南與且末、南與精絕接。

translations.literal: Northeast joins Yanqi; southeast joins Qiemo; south joins Jingjue.
translations.idiomatic: It bordered Yanqi to the northeast, Qiemo to the southeast, and Jingjue on the south.

sentences.zh: 西有河，至龜茲五百八十里。

translations.literal: West has a river; to Qiuci five hundred eighty li.
translations.idiomatic: A river lay to the west; Kucha was five hundred eighty li away.

sentences.zh: 自武帝初通西域、置校尉，屯田渠犁。

translations.literal: From Emperor Wu's first opening the Western Regions and establishing colonels, garrison-fielded at Quli.
translations.idiomatic: Ever since Emperor Wu first opened the Western Regions and posted colonels, Han had farmed garrison lands at Quli.

sentences.zh: 是時，軍旅連出，師行三十二年，海內虛耗。

translations.literal: At this time, armies repeatedly went out; the host marched thirty-two years; within the seas empty and consumed.
translations.idiomatic: Campaigns had followed one another for thirty-two years, draining the empire.

sentences.zh: 征和中，貳師將軍李廣利以軍降匈奴。

translations.literal: In Zhenghe period, Ershi General Li Guangli with the army surrendered to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: During the Zhenghe era Ershi General Li Guangli surrendered his army to the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 上既悔遠征伐，而搜粟都尉桑弘羊與丞相御史奏言：「故輪台東捷枝、渠犁皆故國，地廣，饒水草，有溉田五千頃以上，處溫和，田美，可益通溝渠，種五穀，與中國同時孰。

translations.literal: The sovereign having already regretted distant campaigns, yet Search-for-Grain Commandant Sang Hongyang together with the chancellor and imperial clerk memorialized saying: 'Former east of Luntai Jiezhi and Quli are all old states, land broad, rich in water and grass, having irrigated fields five thousand qing and above, situated mild and warm, fields beautiful, can increase open ditches and canals, plant the five grains, together with the Central States same season ripe.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had repented his distant wars, but Search-for-Grain Commandant Sang Hongyang and the chancellor and imperial counsellor memorialized: 'East of Luntai lie the old domains of Jiezhi and Quli—broad lands, rich pasture, more than five thousand qing of irrigable soil in a mild climate. Ditches could be extended, the five grains sown, and harvests timed with the heartland.'

sentences.zh: 其旁國少錐刀，貴黃金采繒，可以易谷食，宜給足不乏。

translations.literal: Its neighboring states lack awl-knives, prize gold and colored silks; can exchange for grain food; ought to supply enough without shortage.
translations.idiomatic: Neighboring kingdoms lack metal tools yet value gold and brocades, so grain could be traded for cloth and metal until supplies were ample.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為可遣屯田卒詣故輪台以東，置校尉三人分護，各舉圖地形，通利溝渠，務使以時益種五穀，張掖、酒泉遣騎假司馬為斥候，屬校尉，事有便宜，因騎置以聞。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks can dispatch garrison-field soldiers to go to former Luntai eastward, establish three colonels divided to protect, each raise maps terrain, open profitable ditches and canals, strive to cause in season increase plant five grains; Zhangye and Jiuquan dispatch cavalry acting-major as scouts, subordinate to colonels; matters having convenience, by relay post to report.
translations.idiomatic: We propose sending colonists east of old Luntai under three colonels who would survey the ground, cut channels, and expand grain plantings on schedule, while Zhangye and Jiuquan detail acting majors with cavalry as scouts under those colonels, reporting anything urgent by relay.

sentences.zh: 田一歲，有積穀，募民壯健有累重敢徙者詣田所，就畜積為本業，益墾溉田，稍築列亭，連城而西，以威西國，輔烏孫，為便。

translations.literal: Fields one year, having stored grain, recruit people sturdy healthy having burdens daring to move go to field places, approach livestock accumulation as root occupation, increase reclaim irrigate fields, gradually build row watchtowers, connect cities westward, thereby awe the western states, assist Wusun, is convenient.
translations.idiomatic: After one season's crop there would be granaries to draw sturdy settlers with families; they could build on those stores, open more irrigated land, raise a line of signal towers westward in a chain of forts to overawe the western states and support Wusun—altogether the advantageous course.

sentences.zh: 臣謹遣征事臣昌分部行邊，嚴敕太守、都尉明烽火，選士馬，謹斥候，蓄茭草。

translations.literal: Your servant respectfully dispatches investigation officer your servant Chang in sections to go the border, strictly order grand administrators and commandants clarify beacon fires, select soldiers and horses, careful scouts, store fodder grass.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant has sent Investigator Chang to tour the frontier by sectors with orders to the grand administrators and commandants to man the beacons, drill troops and mounts, tighten patrols, and stock fodder.

sentences.zh: 願陛下遣使使西國，以安其意。

translations.literal: Wish Your Majesty dispatch envoys to go to western states, thereby to pacify their minds.
translations.idiomatic: We ask that you send envoys to the western kingdoms to reassure them.

sentences.zh: 臣昧死請。」

translations.literal: Your servant blind to death requests.
translations.idiomatic: We beg leave to speak at the peril of our lives.

sentences.zh: 上乃下詔，深陳既往之悔，曰：

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon issued an edict, deeply stating past regret, saying:
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then promulgated an edict that set forth in painful detail his regret for the past. It read:

sentences.zh: 前有司奏，欲益民賦三十助邊用，是重困老弱孤獨也。

translations.literal: Formerly responsible officials memorialized, wishing to increase people's tax thirty to assist border use; this is heavily to distress old weak orphaned alone.
translations.idiomatic: Not long ago ministers asked to raise the poll tax by thirty cash for the frontier—that would only crush the aged, the weak, and those without kin.

sentences.zh: 而今又請遣卒田輪台。

translations.literal: And now again request dispatch soldiers to field Luntai.
translations.idiomatic: Now they ask again to send troops to farm at Luntai.

sentences.zh: 輪台西於車師千餘里，前開陵侯擊車師時，危須、尉犁、樓蘭六國子弟在京師者皆先歸，發畜食迎漢軍，又自發兵，凡數萬人，王各自將，共圍車師，降其王。

translations.literal: Luntai is west of Jushi more than one thousand li; previously when Marquis of Kaifeng-ling struck Jushi, Weixu, Yanqi, Loulan six states' youths in the capital all first returned, issued livestock food to welcome Han army, again themselves issued troops, in all several tens of thousands, kings each personally led, together surrounded Jushi, submitted its king.
translations.idiomatic: Luntai lies more than a thousand li west of Jushi. When Marquis Kaifeng-ling attacked Jushi, young nobles of six states—Weixu, Yanqi, Loulan, and the rest—who were in the capital hurried home, drove out herds and grain to feed Han troops, and raised tens of thousands of their own horse; each king led his men, and together they besieged Jushi and forced its king to yield.

sentences.zh: 諸國兵便罷，力不能復至道上食漢軍。

translations.literal: The various states' troops then ceased; strength not able again to reach the road above to feed Han army.
translations.idiomatic: Those allies then dispersed and lacked strength to meet our columns on the march with further supplies.

sentences.zh: 漢軍破城，食至多，然士自載不足以竟師，強者盡食畜產，羸者道死數千人。

translations.literal: Han army broke the city; food arrived very much, yet soldiers self-carry not enough to finish the army; strong ones entirely ate livestock property; weak ones on the road died several thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: Han took the town and found ample grain, yet the men could not carry enough to finish the campaign; the strong devoured the livestock and the weak died by thousands along the road.

sentences.zh: 朕發酒泉驢、橐駝負食，出玉門迎軍。

translations.literal: I issued Jiuquan donkeys and camels bearing food, went out Jade Gate to welcome the army.
translations.idiomatic: I ordered donkeys and camels from Jiuquan loaded with grain to meet the army beyond the Jade Gate.

sentences.zh: 吏卒起張掖，不甚遠，然尚廝留其眾。

translations.literal: Officials and soldiers rose from Zhangye, not very far, yet still delayed detaining their masses.
translations.idiomatic: Relief parties set out even from nearby Zhangye, yet still the host was stalled and straggling.

sentences.zh: 曩者，朕之不明，以軍候弘上書言「匈奴縛馬前後足，置城下，馳言『秦人，我匄若馬』」，又漢使者久留不還，故興遣貳師將軍，欲以為使者威重也。

translations.literal: Formerly, I not clear, because army scout Hong submitted a memorial saying 'The Xiongnu bound horses front and rear feet, placed below the city, galloped saying Qin people, I beg your horses,' again Han envoys long detained not returning, therefore raised and dispatched Ershi General, wished thereby for envoys awe and weight.
translations.idiomatic: Once before, misled by a report from the scout Hong that the Xiongnu had hobbled their horses beneath the walls and shouted, 'Men of Qin, we beg your horses,' and because Han envoys had long been detained, I launched the Ershi expedition chiefly to overawe them and ransom our men.

sentences.zh: 古者卿大夫與謀，參以蓍龜，不吉不行。

translations.literal: In antiquity ministers and grandees together deliberated, consulted by milfoil and tortoise; if not auspicious did not proceed.
translations.idiomatic: The ancients took counsel with their high ministers and checked the milfoil and tortoise—if the omens were ill they did not move.

sentences.zh: 乃者以縛馬書遍視丞相、御史、二千石、諸大夫、郎為文學者，乃至郡屬國都尉成忠、趙破奴等，皆以「虜自縛其馬，不祥甚哉！

translations.literal: Recently taking the bound-horse document everywhere showed the chancellor, imperial clerk, two thousand shi, various grandees, gentlemen serving as literary scholars, even to commandant of dependent states Cheng Zhong, Zhao Ponu and others; all with 'The barbarians themselves bound their horses—inauspicious in the extreme!'
translations.idiomatic: Lately I circulated the bound-horse dispatch to the chancellor, counsellor, two-thousand-dan officials, court scholars, and even border officers such as Cheng Zhong and Zhao Ponu; all cried that for the barbarians to hobble their own mounts was the worst of ill omens.

sentences.zh: 」或以為「欲以見強，夫不足者視人有餘。

translations.literal: Some thought 'Wish thereby to display strength; he who is insufficient looks on others as having surplus.'
translations.idiomatic: Others read it as bluster from a people who, knowing their weakness, pretend to strength.

sentences.zh: 」《易》之卦得《大過》，爻在九五，匈奴困敗。

translations.literal: The Changes' hexagram obtained Da Guo; line at nine-five; Xiongnu trapped and defeated.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Changes gave the hexagram Da Guo with a moving line at the fifth place—sure sign that the Xiongnu would be broken.

sentences.zh: 公軍方士、太史治星望氣，及太卜龜蓍，皆以為吉，匈奴必破，時不可再得也。

translations.literal: Dukes' army recipe masters, Grand Clerk managing stars gazing vapors, and Grand Diviner tortoise and milfoil, all thought auspicious; Xiongnu must be broken; the moment cannot be obtained twice.
translations.idiomatic: Court astrologers, star-readers, and the grand diviner with tortoise and stalks alike pronounced the signs favorable and the Xiongnu doomed—a chance not to be missed.

sentences.zh: 又曰：「北伐行將，於釜山必克。

translations.literal: Again said: 'North campaign marching general, at Fushan must conquer.'
translations.idiomatic: They added, 'The northern expedition will prevail on Mount Fu.'

sentences.zh: 」卦諸將，貳師最吉。

translations.literal: Divined the various generals; Ershi most auspicious.
translations.idiomatic: When the generals were divined, Ershi's lot was the best.

sentences.zh: 故朕親發貳師下釜山，詔之必毋深入。

translations.literal: Therefore I personally dispatched Ershi down Fushan, edicting him must not deeply enter.
translations.idiomatic: So I sent Ershi against Mount Fu in person, yet ordered him not to drive too deep.

sentences.zh: 今計謀卦兆皆反繆。

translations.literal: Now plans and divination signs all contrary and wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Now every plan and every omen has proved the reverse of truth.

sentences.zh: 重合侯得虜候者，言：「聞漢軍當來，匈奴使巫埋羊牛所出諸道及水上以詛軍。

translations.literal: Marquis of Chonghe obtained a barbarian scout, who said: 'Hearing Han army about to come, the Xiongnu sent shamans bury sheep and cattle on the various roads where they issue and on the waters above to curse the army.'
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Chonghe captured a Xiongnu scout who said, 'When they heard Han was coming, the Xiongnu set shamans to bury sheep and cattle at every spring and ford to curse your host.'

sentences.zh: 單于遺天子馬裘，常使巫祝之。

translations.literal: The Chanyu presented the Son of Heaven a horse fur robe; constantly sent shamans to curse-pray it.
translations.idiomatic: The chanyu even sent the Son of Heaven a horse-hide cloak and kept shamans working curses on it.

sentences.zh: 縛馬者，詛軍事也。

translations.literal: Binding horses is cursing the army affair.
translations.idiomatic: Hobbling the horses was part of the same curse against our army.

sentences.zh: 」又卜「漢軍一將不吉」。

translations.literal: Again divined 'Han army one general not auspicious.'
translations.idiomatic: The tortoise had also warned that one Han general would meet ill fortune.

sentences.zh: 匈奴常言：「漢極大，然不能飢渴，失一狼，走千羊。」

translations.literal: The Xiongnu constantly say: 'Han is extremely great, yet it cannot endure hunger and thirst; lose one wolf, a thousand sheep flee.'
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu have a saying: 'Han is vast, yet it cannot go hungry and thirsty; let one wolf slip away and you lose a thousand sheep.'

sentences.zh: 乃者貳師敗，軍士死略離散，悲痛常在朕心。

translations.literal: Lately Ershi was defeated; soldiers died, were captured, scattered; grief and pain constantly remain in my heart.
translations.idiomatic: Since Ershi's defeat I have lived with the grief of men killed, taken, or scattered in rout.

sentences.zh: 今請遠田輪台，欲起亭隧，是擾勞天下，非所以優民也。

translations.literal: Now they request distant fielding at Luntai, wish to raise watchtowers and beacon tunnels; this is to disturb and toil all under heaven; it is not the means to treat the people kindly.
translations.idiomatic: To farm distant Luntai now and raise beacons and tunnels would harry the empire and is no way to show mercy to the people.

sentences.zh: 今朕不忍聞。

translations.literal: Now I cannot bear to hear it.
translations.idiomatic: I cannot bear to hear more of this.

sentences.zh: 大鴻臚等又議，欲募囚徒送匈奴使者，明封侯之賞以報忿，五伯所弗能為也。

translations.literal: The Grand Herald and others again deliberated, wishing to recruit convicts to deliver Xiongnu envoys, clarifying marquis enfeoffment rewards to repay resentment—what the Five Overlords could not do.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Herald and his colleagues even proposed ransoming rage with convict escorts and promised marquisates—something the Five Hegemons themselves would not have attempted.

sentences.zh: 且匈奴得漢降者，常提掖搜索，問以所聞。

translations.literal: Moreover the Xiongnu, when they obtain Han deserters, constantly lift and search them, asking what they have heard.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, whenever the Xiongnu take Han defectors they strip and search them and wring out every rumor.

sentences.zh: 今邊塞未正，闌出不禁，障候長吏使卒獵獸，以皮肉為利，卒苦而烽火乏，失亦上集不得，後降者來，若捕生口虜，乃知之。

translations.literal: Now the border passes are not rectified; sneaking out is not forbidden; barrier-post long clerks dispatch soldiers to hunt beasts, taking skin and flesh for profit; the soldiers suffer while beacon fires lack fuel; if losses mount they cannot be reported upward; when later deserters come, it is like capturing live barbarians—only then does one know.
translations.idiomatic: The passes are still slack, illegal crossings go unchecked, and garrison officers send men hunting for hides and meat while the men suffer and the beacon fuel runs short; losses never reach my desk, and I learn the truth only when fresh deserters arrive as living witnesses.

sentences.zh: 當今務在禁苛暴，止擅賦，力本農，修馬復令，以補缺，毋乏武備而已。

translations.literal: The pressing task of the moment lies in forbidding harsh violence, stopping arbitrary levies, putting strength into root agriculture, repairing the horse exemption order, thereby to fill shortages—only do not let martial readiness fail.
translations.idiomatic: Our duty now is to curb cruelty, end illegal taxes, put effort into farming, restore the horse-furlough statutes to make up shortfalls, and keep defenses sound—nothing more.

sentences.zh: 郡國二千石各上進畜馬方略補邊狀，與計對。

translations.literal: Commanderies and kingdoms' two-thousand-dan each submit upward plans for raising livestock and horses to replenish the border situation, to pair with the annual accounting.
translations.idiomatic: Let every two-thousand-dan official submit plans for breeding horses and stock for the frontier to be filed with the annual accounts.

sentences.zh: 由是不復出軍。

translations.literal: From this time no longer dispatched armies.
translations.idiomatic: After that no further armies were sent.

sentences.zh: 而封丞相車千秋為富民侯，以明休息，思富養民也。

translations.literal: Thereupon enfeoffed Chancellor Che Qianqiu as Marquis of Enriching the People, thereby to clarify rest and repose, thinking to enrich and nurture the people.
translations.idiomatic: The court enfeoffed Chancellor Che Qianqiu as marquis of Enriching the People to signal a turn toward rest and toward enriching the common people.

sentences.zh: 初，貳師將軍李廣利擊大宛，還過杅彌，杅彌遣太子賴丹為質於龜茲。

translations.literal: At first, Ershi General Li Guangli struck Dayuan; returning he passed Wumi; Wumi dispatched Crown Prince Laidan as hostage at Qiuci.
translations.idiomatic: When Ershi General Li Guangli marched against Dayuan he passed through Wumi, which sent Crown Prince Laidan as a hostage lodged in Kucha.

sentences.zh: 廣利責電茲曰：「外國皆臣屬於漢，龜茲何以得受杅彌質？

translations.literal: Guangli reproached Dianzi, saying: 'Foreign states all are subjects to Han; how can Qiuci obtain accepting Wumi's hostage?'
translations.idiomatic: Guangli rebuked the king of Kucha—the text writes Dianzi—demanding, 'Every outer state acknowledges Han; by what right did Kucha accept Wumi's hostage?'

sentences.zh: 」即將賴丹入至京師。

translations.literal: Thereupon led Laidan in to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: He marched Laidan straight to the capital.

sentences.zh: 昭帝乃用桑弘羊前議，以杅彌太子賴丹為校尉，將軍田輪台，輪台與渠犁地皆相連也。

translations.literal: Emperor Zhao thereupon used Sang Hongyang's former proposal, taking Wumi Crown Prince Laidan as colonel, general fielding Luntai; Luntai and Quli lands both connect with each other.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhao revived Sang Hongyang's plan, named the Wumi crown prince Laidan a colonel, and set him to colonize Luntai, whose fields adjoined Quli.

sentences.zh: 龜茲貴人姑翼謂其王曰：「賴丹本臣屬吾國，今佩漢印綬來，迫吾國而田，必為害。

translations.literal: A Qiuci noble Guyi said to his king: 'Laidan originally was subject to our state; now he comes bearing Han seal and cord, pressing our state to field crops—this will surely be harmful.'
translations.idiomatic: A Kucha noble named Guyi warned his king, 'Laidan was once our vassal; now he returns wearing Han's seal ribbon to force our soil under the plow—that can only harm us.'

sentences.zh: 」王即殺賴丹，而上書謝漢，漢未能征。

translations.literal: The king thereupon killed Laidan, and upward submitted a memorial apologizing to Han; Han was not able to punish.
translations.idiomatic: The king murdered Laidan and sent an apology to Han, which was in no position to retaliate.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時，長羅侯常惠使烏孫還，便宜發諸國兵，合五萬人攻龜茲，責以前殺校尉賴丹。

translations.literal: In Emperor Xuan's time, Marquis of Changluo Chang Hui, returning from mission to Wusun, on convenience raised various states' troops, together fifty thousand men, attacked Qiuci, reproaching them for the earlier killing of Colonel Laidan.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan, Marquis Changluo Chang Hui, returning from Wusun, mustered fifty thousand allied troops on his own authority and struck Kucha to punish the murder of Colonel Laidan.

sentences.zh: 龜茲王謝曰：「乃我先王時為貴人姑翼所誤，我無罪。

translations.literal: The Qiuci king apologized, saying: 'It was in my former king's time, mistaken by the noble Guyi; I am without guilt.'
translations.idiomatic: The king of Kucha answered, 'That crime belongs to my father's reign and the mischief of the noble Guyi; I am innocent.'

sentences.zh: 」執姑翼詣惠，惠斬之。

translations.literal: Seized Guyi and brought him to Hui; Hui beheaded him.
translations.idiomatic: He handed Guyi over to Chang Hui, who executed him.

sentences.zh: 時烏孫公主遣女來至京師學鼓琴，漢遣侍郎樂奉送主女，過龜茲。

translations.literal: At the time the Wusun princess sent her daughter to come to the capital to study plucked zither; Han dispatched Palace Attendant Yue to escort the princess's daughter, passing Qiuci.
translations.idiomatic: The Wusun princess had sent her daughter to Chang'an to study the zither; Gentleman Yue was detailed to escort the girl home through Kucha.

sentences.zh: 龜茲前遣人至烏孫求公主女，未還。

translations.literal: Qiuci earlier had dispatched people to Wusun to seek the princess's daughter; they had not yet returned.
translations.idiomatic: Kucha had already sent envoys to Wusun to ask for the princess's daughter, and they had not yet come back.

sentences.zh: 會女過龜茲，龜茲王留不遣，復使使報公主，主許之。

translations.literal: When the girl passed Qiuci, the Qiuci king detained her and would not send her on; again sent envoys to report to the princess; the princess permitted it.
translations.idiomatic: When the girl reached Kucha the king held her back and messaged the princess, who consented.

sentences.zh: 後公主上書，願令女比宗室入朝，而龜茲王絳賓亦受其夫人，上書言得尚漢外孫為昆弟，願與公主女俱入朝。

translations.literal: Afterward the princess submitted a memorial, wishing to order the daughter to enter court like the imperial lineage, while also the Qiuci king Jiangbin accepted his consort's view, submitted a memorial saying he had obtained marrying Han's maternal granddaughter as elder-younger brother, wished together with the princess's daughter both to enter court.
translations.idiomatic: The princess then asked that her daughter be allowed to attend court as if of the imperial clan; King Jiangbin of Kucha, swayed by his queen, added his own plea to marry a Han imperial granddaughter in fraternal alliance and to bring both women to the capital.

sentences.zh: 元康元年，遂來朝賀。

translations.literal: In Yuankang first year, thereupon they came to court for congratulations.
translations.idiomatic: In the first year of Yuankang they arrived for the New Year audience.

sentences.zh: 王及夫人皆賜印綬。

translations.literal: The king and consort were both gifted seals and cords.
translations.idiomatic: King and queen each received seal and ribbon.

sentences.zh: 夫人號稱公主，賜以車騎旗鼓，歌吹數十人，綺繡雜繒琦珍凡數千萬。

translations.literal: The consort was titled and called princess; granted chariots, cavalry, drums, and banners, several tens of singers and players, brocades and mixed silks and rare treasures in all several tens of millions.
translations.idiomatic: The queen was styled princess and was given chariots, escort, drums, and dozens of musicians, with brocades, silks, and curios worth tens of millions.

sentences.zh: 留且一年，厚贈送之。

translations.literal: Detained nearly one year; thickly gifted and sent them off.
translations.idiomatic: They stayed almost a year before the court sent them home with lavish gifts.

sentences.zh: 後數來朝賀，樂漢衣服制度，歸其國，治宮室，作檄道周衛，出入傳呼，撞鐘鼓，如漢家儀。

translations.literal: Afterward several times came to court for congratulations, delighted in Han clothing and institutions; returning to their state, they built palace chambers, made patrol roads with surrounding guards, going out and in with shouted clearance, striking bells and drums, like Han household ritual.
translations.idiomatic: They returned often for audiences, copied Han dress and court style, rebuilt their palace, ran cordoned carriage roads with heralds and bell-and-drum salutes like a Han mansion.

sentences.zh: 外國胡人皆曰：「驢非驢，馬非馬，若龜茲王，所謂騾也。

translations.literal: Foreign Hu people all said: 'Not donkey, not horse—like the king of Qiuci, what is called a mule.'
translations.idiomatic: The frontier Hu joked, 'Neither ass nor horse—just like the king of Kucha: a mule.'

sentences.zh: 」絳賓死，其子丞德自謂漢外孫，成、哀帝時往來尤數，漢遇之亦甚親密。

translations.literal: Jiangbin died; his son Chengde called himself Han's maternal grandson; in Cheng and Ai emperors' times comings and goings were especially frequent; Han treated him also very intimately.
